item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't it just so convenient when some big idea you had on the backboard fits perfectly with the newest hit contest? #MassaTruthetts has been on my idea shelf for a long while. It just so happened to be perfect for this contest. It is historical for Massachusetts, and the United States, and the Foundation as a whole.

How improbable…

item.title: Novote
item.content:encoded:
It's so, so close to being a +1, but I just cannot abide by the final dropdown. The story just ending with Massachusetts no longer being anomalous because the foundation made this collective mistake into a reality is good. It works as a fully self contained story.

Using this as a way to say "Hey, isn't all this OTHER stuff also really unlikely and crazy????" feels silly and unneccary to me. Not everything needs to be meta. I think removing that tab at the end would do nothing but improve this SCP. If this ever gets changed in some way, I'd say that warrants an upvote, but for now it's just a good story with an awkward note tacked on the end that adds nothing of value.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably because it contained mainland Massachusetts and not it’s expansion, and that is why the affects did not reach Maine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems a bit weird to me that this article doesn't mention Maine at all even though it was part of Massachusetts until 1820. Like, how did that work?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this instilled the fear/relief that my trips to boston weren't real.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The punchline is a little corny, but works. It seems that someone had to wonder why the veil has existed with all the crazy SCPs throughout history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted simply for the bonkers idea that the foundation recreated Massachusetts from scratch,and that it explains the foundation having enough land to house such a big organization.
That said, the initial explanation of the perception of Massachusetts colonist was convoluted and boiled down to a border misunderstanding which i wanna say i read happen in RL in some other place similarly. Not too anomalous.
I do like that the article raises the question, if the foundation recrested the state does it make more real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some stuff about this doesn't really make sense. Massachusetts in the real world doesn't any more "exist" than the borders and territory we assigned to it. There's nothing about those ten and a half thousand square miles that are inherently Massachusetts. This idea that colonists came to spots and called them Massachusetts "even though they weren't in Massachusetts" just means that Massachusetts is in a different place and/or covers a different area than seen on maps. Whatever the colonists decided was Massachusetts was Massachusetts. That's how states are created.

Also rolling MassaTruthetts into this possible Veil anomaly makes for a great plot twist, but not so much a great article. A big point in the true article is "the Veil, false-4006 included, might not exist", even though the false article is way too much of a massive coincidence to not be some form of antimemetic anomaly. The Greg thing became part of the log of extranormal events, and this is much bigger. A better course of action would have been to have two separate SCPs, and use MassaTruthetts (known anomaly) as evidence for the Veil (supposed anomaly), and I know that wouldn't have worked for a 4000 proposal, I get it.

Yes, to you MassaTruthetts is a part of the Veil, because you're the writer. But to the Foundation it's still only evidence for the Veil's existence. "The public's ignorance of Massachusetts" being part of "The public's ignorance of anomalies as a whole" may be true, but the Foundation doesn't know that for sure according to the article, making the choice by the Foundation to have it all as one article nonsensical. To put it another way, were the Foundation to decide that true-4006, being only theoretical, was in fact not a real anomaly, then it would also be deciding that false-4006 was all a big coincidence, which is sort of aggressively lolFoundation. Two articles would have better suited this SCP.

Despite this enormous critical comment, I really did enjoy the SCP, and I'm novoting it just because it breaks suspension of disbelief, structurally, in a way that probably wasn't what you intended. Great concept, great execution, and the whole "Foundation protecting and inadvertently creating Massachusetts as the world moves into the modern age" was very well done, especially since it's implied it might not even have been necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, I havent logged into here in months, hopefully I remember to post this correctly, but wow. This made me laugh really hard, and I mean that as a compliment. As a (supposed, I guess according to this article :P) resident of the state its really cool to see a SCP like this. I feel like I'm having trouble articulating it well but I really love this. Clearly a lot of thought was put into it to make it fit into the SCP universe. +1
Thank you all so much for gifting me my first +300 :D I don't have much more to say than that.

item.title: Re: why..
item.content:encoded: Simple: We already know that Wyoming doesn't exist!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Besides SCP-4001 probably the best Series 5 I've seen so far

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is definitely one of the most creative articles I’ve read in a long time. I actually think the collapse was a good addition to the article, and as a resident of Massachusetts potential SCP-4006-A instance, I thought this entire article was a good balance of subtle humor and classic Foundation clinical tone.

+1

item.title: Re: why..
item.content:encoded: I literally did Massachusetts for the pun of #MassaTruthetts. That's just the way the cookie crumbles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: Oh dear lord ignore this. I don't know how I fucked that up that bad. Meant to reply to someone on page 4.

item.title: why..
item.content:encoded: Why didn't you do this on Wyoming it would've been hilarious

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I live in massachusetts, so this SCP was very entertaining.

item.title: My First +200
item.content:encoded: Yayy! Thank y'all for this~! Don't really have much else to say. But hurray! +200!

item.title: Genius
item.content:encoded:
This made me laugh my a** off. Do a follow-up for North Dakota!
You know anyone from North Dakota? Me neither!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This guy gets it :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clearly, those SCPs aren't actually in Massachusetts, but a shockingly unlikely series of clerical errors and mistaken observations by Foundation personnel have resulted in all of them being recorded as being in Massachusetts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably one of the best SCPs I've read. This explains every single "unrealistic" concept or notion of every other SCP, and the whole foundation itself, especially on how the SCP foundation can be so large, yet be kept secret.

I SMASHED that +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who currently lives in Massachusetts (as far as I know o_0) I found this very entertaining, it actually made me chuckle out loud. It's like the "Finland conspiracy" applied to Mass.

One question though: how does this jive with SCP-2480 and others set in Mass?

I agree with the consensus that the level 5 clearance section isn't really needed.

In any case, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …you have done terrible things to my brain…+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My fiancee lives in Massachusetts.

I knew she was too good to be true. (+1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh you exist…

…Just not where you think you exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm now proud to have been told by the site I hold so dear that I don't actually exist. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Contrary to what many of the opinions above, I was less a fan of the Massachusetts-specific anomaly, but the collapsible hooked me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed with the dislike of the collapsible. No-voting; it feels very forced even if it kind of makes sense.

You're essentially just dropping a stinger and let it speak for itself, but it doesn't actually explain itself. It makes me feel like I should have read something else to understand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a Bostonian, you get my upvote.

item.title: Re: Reaaally not what I expected.
item.content:encoded: Of course, I do understand. That's a minor peeve with the article with regards to the contest, the concept itself is great.

item.title: Re: Reaaally not what I expected.
item.content:encoded: I concede to the America centric theme. It’s just the history I’m most familiar with — and the article originated with the pun #MassaTruthetts so it’s not like that could work any other way. American because I’m American. Just the way it worked out.

item.title: Reaaally not what I expected.
item.content:encoded:
++ I don't get it. I mean, I get the offset and everything it implies, it's just...
++ Either I'm not smart enough, or this is a comment from Massachusetts, which means you should ignore it altogether.

This is cool and engaging… but confusing. It might be just me not getting it at all, but I feel it breaks down badly when in-universe logic is applied to it. As I read it, I kept expecting to have it going "oh, hey, this is gonna turn out to be a MASSIVE internal screw the Foundation has been turning inside its own institutional head, because Massachusetts is a real place with real people, it's just their personnel that keep missing them via this anomaly and a few memeticists went and said WELL DAMN WE BETTER DO SOMETHING ABOUT THIS until they managed to make everybody believe in SCP-4000, then introduce them to the Explained 4000 and voilá! Anomaly neutralized, Foundation goes back to normal… as it were", but it just went somewhere else, and it didn't go where I expected in a way I found a bit deflated.

Another thing is the mechanism; this is either probabilistic or probabilistic and memetic; that is, the universe conspires to keep Massachusetts from actually being populated (including people somehow creating the word Massachusetts; if this is probabilistic, it means some colonizer just wanted to talk about a mass going and couldn't choose which church they preferred, but somebody else just misheard and thought they talked about zat darned plaze ze English already colonized! Sacrebleu!)1 or the universe conspires to leep Massachusetts from actually being populated AND the rest of the world understands and accepts the fact that all these public representatives, personalities, celebrities, TV shows, nation-relevant affairs, produce and friggin' train tracks are from Massachusetts and not from the place(s) they're actually from.

Yes, the Foundation would fabricate a lot of things. They would pay through their wide noses to get a Foundation owned-state, and would happily and proudly release it from containment once they normalized it. The article is well-written and beautiful… but there are just many people not noticing the damn thing is fake for it to be only probabilistic.

Despite all peeves mentioned in the paragraphs upstairs, it is elegantly written and a perfect example of what Foundation writers should aspire to, myself included. Therefore, no-vote; I reserve my right to change it on re-read, but besides all of this, it is just not what I'd think a world-spanning community dedicated to fiction should keep as the go-to history SCP article, it being entirely about the United States.2

tl;dr Not quite what I expected; it was very good, all things considered, but I couldn't bring myself to enjoy it due to over-analyzing it. Sorry about that. No-vote, but I'll keep an eye on it.

Footnotes
1. I'm aware French people don't think like that nor exist. My apologies for a very silly joke.
2. You could say this is because I'm not American, but Spanish, and definitely do exist. Unlike the French, I mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The lines are blurred. It’s hard to know when the Foundation stopped being the public, or when, say, the CIA, the FBI, or the MI5 didn’t. But at some point, clearly they became something else that works around (and within) the anomaly. Of course, if people want to write about the intricacies of implications here, I think that’d be neat. I’m not planning on doing that myself though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so clever. I love the mental gymnastics you put the reader through. The Foundation ultimately concluding that it would be best to assume the anomaly just heavily documented doesn't exist is a great touch.

I get that this anomaly keeps anomalous things from the public, and not from the Foundation because they aren't the public. But wouldn't those who started the Foundation, or those about the first recorded anomaly have been the public at one point? It implies that there's an almost innate difference between Foundation employees/participants and the civilian population, and I don't know how that could be.

Yay!!

My first 100% solo article in a while to reach +100! Maybe I really am a good writer that can stand up on their own without a collab, would you look at that. +10 Self Esteem :O

And yes, I waited until we were at +101 to make this post because I wanted a buffer so that a stray downvote couldn't get this back under +100 while this post was up. If it still drops down like that now I'm gon' be a little mad. But ah well. Yay +100 for #MassaTruthetts!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this. I don't think it broke my suspension of disbelief, there are much larger scale articles on this site. It's a creative idea, and the reveal at the end (while it took me a few seconds to process it) is satisfying and the possible multiple implications of the veil itself being an anomaly is something worth exploring.

Solid +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact that Massachusetts was never populated was extraordinarily weird. Thus, SCP-4000, the veil, hid it like it would any other anomaly. The beginnings of Massachusetts being uninhabited might have been the doing of some other anomaly, or might have been just what it was: a series of crazy coincidences. With an anomaly like SCP-4000, it’s hzrd to know what we’re real coincidences and what was it’s doing:

Where coincidences ended and SCP-4000’s influence began on the Massschusettd story is hard to know.

However, the Foundation is able to break this veil because they are not the public, for which the veil is maintained. SCP-4000 does not work on the Foundation in the way it works on the rest of the people, because the Foundation might itself be part of SCP-4000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So was SCP-4000 (Veil) covering our entire planet, but it was covering Massachusetts even more strongly? Until the Foundation definitively broke the extra strong Massachusetts Veil via exposing the public to the area too much, causing it to return to regular Earth Veil strength?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it a lot up until the O5 classified part. After that, it was a bit too 'meta` and confusing for me. For me, I dont neccessarily like it as a X000 article, but without the last O5 part, and maybe with it being explained or neutralised, it would be a good 4XXX article. Was really liking the idea of it being explained. For me, a novote as an X000, but as a 4XXX, probably would upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But do you really

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is basically the Finland conspiracy in SCP form and I love it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I first read this in Logan Airport as well at like, 6:30 AM or 7 AM and the feeling I got from it was absolutely surreal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jeez. Reading this while sitting IN LOGAN AIRPORT. Wow.

What is the foundation hiding from us before constructing Massachusetts into how we perceive it? If the whole place is only a large wasteland mistaken by the world as a populated area, why must the foundation make the delusion real? I mean, the resource and time required to get rid of the communities of 4000-A as well as "neutralizing" the 4000 effect don't really seem like worth it unless there's even more hiding behind the whole document.
I kinda got the point that this entry is more or less a "veil of ignorance" stuff, but… there's a subtle difference between people perceiving some nonexistent stuff as real and people having no idea about all the dirty stuff the foundation gets its hands on.

But yeah… When I first got my eyes upon the document I was filled with a strange feeling of "who am I and where am I".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is, without question, my favorite Thaumiel ever.

Plus I have very conflicting memories of a childhood visit to Cape Cod.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was woah in a good way. I think I get what you're saying with the second page, but I'm not sure.

My Guess
My Guess

Is that 4000 is either a divine being or normalcy itself.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the misconception here is "both of these anomalies". No two anomalies share one slot. This is all the very same thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The stretching of suspension of disbelief was this article’s whole goal. Well, among other things. But I think breaking suspension of disbelief, and then still earning an upvote? That’s an ultimate win for this article. I’m happy to hear this reaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Urgh. This stretched my suspension of disbelief to the point where it snapped in two. But at the same time, that's my fault as a reader, and this concept and its execution is too clever not to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting.

I get a lot of vibes of SCP-2000 as well as SCP-1422 from this article and think the idea of an entire state being this massive probabilistic anomaly has a fair amount going for it. That this then extends to the concept of the veil itself is a bit of a stretch, but I can buy it.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone from CT I 100% support the idea of MA being a myth, like Australia.
The description gets right to the point, love that. If it was written any other I would've downvoted it for being too confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is cohesion here. The implication at the end of the article is the very same probabilistic anomaly that hid the fact that no one lived in Massachusetts (an occurrence extremely abnormal) from the public. The Foundation scientists were able to break the veil of normalcy because they aren’t the public, and, perhaps, the SCP Foundation itself is part of SCP-4000.

The idea was that this article showcases SCP-4000, the true one, at its most overt, and most prevalent. But in light of the circumstances, the true anomaly is theorised to be much bigger. Why has the whole world not become used to anomalies by this point, how is it even possible to cotton everything before the veil becomes irreparable?

Well, maybe there’s an algorithm. Maybe there’s a method to the madness. Maybe the same probabilistic anomaly that kept the public from knowing the strange nature of Massachusetts… keeps the public from knowing the strange nature of everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not entirely sure I've got this straight. So the idea is that Massachusetts didn't really exist but people thought it did and there were all sorts of details about it (kind of a Samothrace scenario), and the Foundation took the land where nobody was and used it as a base of operations because everyone who thought they lived there lived somewhere else, so there wasn't any danger of people coming to it. Eventually they ended up defictionalizing the state just by operating in it (I like the notion that everyone in MA is actually a Foundation employee, because that means I'm a Foundation employee). So the anomaly was that nobody knew about it or lived in it (and people who thought they lived there somehow only ever encountered other people who thought they lived there), but people started moving there, and now the anomaly is pretty much neutralized?

And then it turns out the real anomaly is that the world has somehow managed to remain unaware of anomalies…which has something to do with Massachusetts? The "true document" doesn't really seem like an alternate version of the "false document" here, more like a separate thing entirely. Is the idea of the veil being anomalous such a protected secret that the Foundation higher-ups decided to hide it behind a possibly-neutralized unrelated anomaly, where nobody would look? If the veil is the anomaly, then what does this have to do with history? Maybe the history of MA really was false, but it becomes not the central focus of the article, and thus thematically unfitting for the contest, I would think.

(On a more lighthearted note: if any of this is true, what the hell are the Boston Red Sox‽)

I'm biased towards any article that's about my home state, but unless I'm just particularly dense (which is also 100% possible), I don't think this has any cohesion to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent excellent excellent. If I could give this multiple upvotes I would. Good job!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with TL and Deci here -

• Opinions
◦ CEASE TO OPINE

This doesn't feel like a single anomaly so much as two similar anomalies thrown together, and I don't think that either anomaly is particularly moving on its own. The Foundation building the entire state of Massachusetts to match people's anomalous delusions feels unjustified, even if I assume that the Foundation has that kind of power in the first place. I can't imagine them choosing such a massive effort over simply following their standard protocol of watching for and suppressing information leaks, especially given that the anomaly appears to have remained undiscovered for centuries beforehand without the Foundation's help.

More importantly, the O5 log feels very out-of-the-blue. Unless there were some extremely-subtle hints that I didn't pick up on, there was no indication in the first section of the article that the anomaly was in any way related to the Veil. Because of that, the O5 section seems like a very sudden and drastic non-sequitor - if SCP-4000 is the Veil, then why did I just spend 25 paragraphs reading about Massachusetts? You make both out to be anomalously low-probability sequences of events, but that is a fairly tenuous thread to tie together otherwise-unrelated SCPs.

Were this just the Massachusetts section, I would put it in the "upvote after the contest" category. As is, -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It used to be an offset, it is now a collapsible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, it was well written and kept my attention throughout. I also see you removed the 4/4000 Clearance part, which is good. The O5 'offset' (I guess that's what it's called?) really didn't bother me, I kind of expected it to be there anyway due to the nature of the scp. So my suspension of disbelief is was sustained throughout. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely agree that the offset felt unnecessary and kind of tacked on; it didn't completely kill the article for me, but I definitely think it would be better off without it.

+1 none the less, probably my favorite 4000 entry so far (though i'm admittedly biased cause i live in Mass and love 1912-J)
